1: Isaiah 39:1 introduces the Babylonian envoys who are the pivot between the historical narrative of Isaiah 36-38 and the exile-comfort of Isaiah 40-66: 'baet hahi shalach Merodach-baladan ben-Baladan melek Bavel, at that time Merodach-baladan son of Baladan king of Babylon sent.' Merodach-baladan (Marduk-apla-iddina II in Akkadian) was the Chaldean tribal chief who twice seized Babylon against Assyrian control: 721-710 BCE (during Sargon II's reign) and briefly in 703 BCE (during Sennacherib's early reign). He was a persistent anti-Assyrian resistance leader who sought alliances with western states (including Egypt, Judah, and Elam) to build a coalition against Assyria. His sending of 'letters and a gift' (sefarim umminchah, letters and a gift-offering) to Hezekiah was diplomatically motivated: 'because he had heard that he had been sick and had recovered' — the recovery from illness provided the diplomatic occasion for an alliance-building embassy.
2: 'Hezekiah received them gladly and showed them the storehouse of his treasures' — the fatal pride: 'vayishmach aleihem Chizkiyahu, and Hezekiah rejoiced/was glad over them.' The vayishmach (he was glad = the verb of joyful reception) signals the theological problem: Hezekiah's gladness is in the international attention, not in YHWH who had just saved him from both Sennacherib and his own death. 'He showed them the house of his treasures' (vayar'em et-beit nekhoto, and he showed them his house of precious things) — the entire wealth of the kingdom displayed: 'the silver, the gold, the spices, the fine oil, his entire armory, everything found in his storerooms; there was nothing in his palace or in all his realm that Hezekiah did not show them.' The comprehensive display (nothing not shown) is the precise opposite of the appropriate reserve. Hezekiah uses the occasion of the recovery-visit to show off the kingdom's wealth to a potential military ally — the same strategic calculation (seeking human alliance) that Isaiah had condemned in the Egypt-alliance oracles of Isaiah 30-31.
3: 'Then the prophet Isaiah came to King Hezekiah and asked him: What did these men say, and where did they come from?' — the prophetic interrogation: 'vayyavo Yesha'yahu hannavi el-hammelek Chizkiyahu, and Isaiah the prophet came to King Hezekiah.' Unlike the previous interactions (where Hezekiah sent to Isaiah), here Isaiah comes uninvited — the prophet initiates the confrontation. 'What did these men say?' (mah amru ha'anashim ha'elleh, what did these men say?) and 'where did they come from?' (ume'ayin yavo'u eleykha, and from where did they come to you?) — two simple questions that invite Hezekiah's self-incriminating answer. 'They came from a far country — from Babylon' (meerets rechokah bau mimBavel, from a far land they came, from Babylon) — the 'far country' (erets rechokah) echoes the 'LORD comes from far away' of Isaiah 30:27 (YHWH's approach) but here it is the enemy who comes from far away. Babylon was approximately 900 miles from Jerusalem.
4: 'Isaiah asked: What have they seen in your palace? Hezekiah answered: They have seen everything. There is nothing in my storehouses that I did not show them' — the self-revelation: 'mah rau beveitekha, what did they see in your house?' The simple question exposes the full extent of the indiscretion. 'Everything' (et-kol asher beveyti, everything that is in my house) — the categorical 'everything' is the problem. No diplomatic reserve, no selective disclosure — the entire kingdom's strategic and material assets revealed to a foreign power. 'There is nothing in my storehouses that I did not show them' (lo-hiyah davar asher lo-hare'itim beotsrotai, there was nothing I did not show them in my treasuries) — the emphatic double negative makes the completeness of the disclosure absolute. The king has given the enemy complete strategic intelligence of his kingdom's resources.
5: 'Then Isaiah said to Hezekiah: Hear the word of the LORD of hosts' — the prophetic announcement of judgment: 'vayomer Yesha'yahu el-Chizkiyahu shema debar-YHWH tseva'ot, and Isaiah said to Hezekiah: hear the word of YHWH of hosts.' The 'hear the word of the LORD' (shema debar-YHWH) = the formal prophetic announcement-formula that introduces a divine oracle (1 Kings 22:19; Jeremiah 2:4; Isaiah 7:2; Isaiah 17:20; Isaiah 19:3; Amos 7:16). The use of YHWH tseva'ot (YHWH of hosts = YHWH of armies, the divine warrior title) in this context of a military-political blunder is pointed: the God of armies is about to announce the consequence of trusting in a foreign army's diplomacy rather than in YHWH of armies.
6: 'Watch — days are coming when everything in your palace, everything your ancestors stored up until today, will be carried off to Babylon. Nothing will be left, declares the LORD' — the Babylonian exile predicted: 'hineh yamim ba'im venissa kol-asher beveitekha, behold days are coming and all that is in your house will be carried away.' The hineh yamim ba'im (behold days are coming = the standard prophetic introduction of a future oracle of doom, appearing in Jeremiah 7:32; Isaiah 9:25; Isaiah 31:27-38; Amos 4:2; Isaiah 8:11) introduces the exile oracle. 'Everything your ancestors stored up until today' (ve'et asher aseru avoteykha ad-hayyom hazeh, and what your fathers stored up until this day) = the cumulative wealth of the Davidic dynasty, generations of accumulation — all will be taken. 'Carried off to Babylon' (elBavel yubbel) = the specific destination. This prediction, made in the Assyrian period (701 BCE), points nearly 115 years into the future (586 BCE) to the Babylonian destruction of Jerusalem — the theological bridge between First and Second Isaiah.
7: 'And some of your own sons — your own descendants, whom you will father — will be taken away and serve as court officials in the palace of the king of Babylon' — the deportation of the royal family: 'umibbanekha asher yets'u mimmmekha, and from your sons who will come from you.' 'Whom you will father' (asher tolid) = not yet born at the time of the oracle. 'Will be taken away and become eunuchs' (yuqqachu vehayu sarisim, they will be taken and will be court officials/eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon). The saris (court official/eunuch = the castrated male courtiers who served in Babylonian royal courts; the practice was common in ANE palace administration). Daniel 1:3-7 narrates the fulfillment: 'the king told Ashpenaz, his chief official, to bring some of the Israelites from the royal family and the nobility — young men without any physical defect, handsome, showing aptitude for learning' — Daniel, Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah among them.
8: 'Then Hezekiah said to Isaiah: The word of the LORD that you have spoken is good. And he added: At least there will be peace and security during my own lifetime' — Hezekiah's disturbing acceptance: 'vayomer Chizkiyahu el-Yesha'yahu tov davar-YHWH asher dibbart, and Hezekiah said to Isaiah: the word of YHWH which you have spoken is good.' The acceptance of the oracle as 'good' (tov = the standard of divine evaluation from Gen 1) shows Hezekiah's submission to prophetic authority. But the second clause complicates it: 'ki yehi shalom ve'emet biyamai, for there will be peace and truth in my days.' The 'at least in my own lifetime' = a narrow self-concern that contrasts sharply with Hezekiah's earlier prayer 'so that all the kingdoms of the earth may know that you, LORD, are God' (Isaiah 37:20). There, the prayer was universal; here, the concern is personal. Many interpreters read this as a moral failure; others read it as resigned acceptance of divine decree. In either case, it pivots the narrative toward the exile-comfort of Isaiah 40-66.
